title: Adaptive Real-Time Magnetic Field Tracking beyond Prior Waveform Constraints
authors: Yihan Wang, Xiaofeng Jin, Yuchuan Ming, Jianxiang Miao, Xiao-Ming Lu, M. W. Mitchell, Jia Kong
abstract: The extraction of weak signals plays a crucial role in quantum precision measurement, where the estimation results are often limited by low signal-to-noise ratios. Here, we demonstrate a parameter-estimation framework based on the adaptive extended Kalman filter for dynamic magnetic-field estimation in quantum systems using spin-noise measurements -- a challenging regime characterized by weak signals. By modeling the magnetic field as an unknown parameter, the proposed approach alleviates model dependence in state estimation. Furthermore, by introducing an adaptive algorithm with real-time noise estimation, our method overcomes the measurement noise intensity constraints of conventional extended Kalman filtering and enhances its practical applicability. Numerical simulations covering three representative magnetic-field dynamics validate the capability of the proposed framework, while experimental results demonstrate successful tracking of a seismo-magnetic-like signal beyond the intrinsic sensitivity of conventional spin-noise spectroscopy.
summary: The paper presents an adaptive filtering framework designed to track dynamic magnetic fields in real-time using spin-noise spectroscopy. By dynamically estimating measurement noise, the method overcomes the limitations of traditional filters and can reconstruct weak signals even when the noise levels change abruptly. This allows for the detection of magnetic field variations that are otherwise hidden below the intrinsic sensitivity of standard spectroscopic methods.
main_problem: Extracting weak, time-varying magnetic field signals from high-noise environments where the signal-to-noise ratio is extremely low and the noise statistics may be non-stationary.
main_result: The development of an Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter (AEKF) that successfully tracks dynamic magnetic fields, even with massive measurement noise mismatch and without prior knowledge of the signal waveform, outperforming conventional spin-noise spectroscopy.
method: An Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter (AEKF) utilizing the Sage-Husa algorithm and innovation-based adaptive estimation (IAE) with a sliding window to dynamically update measurement noise covariance.
model_or_system: An 87Rb atomic vapor cell in a magnetic shield using Faraday rotation for detection, where the spin dynamics are governed by the Bloch equations.
key_observables: Photocurrent (capturing polarization fluctuations via the Faraday effect) and Larmor precession frequency.
important_parameters: Larmor frequency, relaxation term, spin-noise fluctuation matrix, and measurement noise intensity.
main_assumptions: The magnetic field is assumed to be a slowly time-varying variable, and measurement noise is assumed to be Gaussian-distributed.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup and agreement between experimental and simulated spin noise spectra; Fig 2 demonstrates AEKF robustness to noise mismatch and sudden disturbances; Fig 3 shows tracking of various field models; Fig 4 presents experimental reconstruction of random-walk and earthquake-like magnetic signals.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of weak signal extraction, proposes the AEKF framework, validates the method through numerical simulations of various field dynamics, and concludes with experimental demonstration using Rb vapor cells.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum sensing and open quantum systems as it provides a robust way to perform real-time parameter estimation in the presence of non-stationary noise and unknown signal dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a)Experimental schematic. The probe light propagates along x-direction, perpendicular to magnetic field oriented along y-direction, through a 87Rb atomic cell. The atoms precess at the Larmor frequency ω, which is set by current-controlled external magnetic field. According to the Faraday effect, spin fluctuations induce S2 Stokes component fluctuations in the probe light, detected by a balanced photodetector and processed by a data acquisition card (DAC). HWP: half wave plate, WP: Wollaston prism. (b)Spin noise spectra (SNS) obtained from both experiment and simulation via fast Fourier transform (FFT), with light blue and red lines representing the respective fitting curves.

Figure 2. (a) Larmor frequency ω generated by Eq.(7) with σ2 = 0.1 as the ground truth (gray solid line), together with the corresponding AEKF estimation result (red solid line) with measurement-noise deviation R′ = 1000. Red-orange, orange and yellow traces show EKF estimates with progressively increased measurement-noise deviations, with values of R′ = 50, 100, and 200, respectively. (b) Squared instantaneous estimation error (∆2ω, red solid line) and the variance estimated by the AEKF (black solid line). (c) Mean squared error (MSE) as a function of the estimation performance used in the EKF and AEKF. We evaluate the average instantaneous variance of simulation data and calculate the...

Figure 3. AEKF-based estimation results for magnetic fields with unknown temporal variations. The true magnetic-field dynamics (gray solid line) and the corresponding AEKF esti- mates (red solid line) are shown for two representative mod- els: (a) an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process and (b) a piece-wise constant field, demonstrating good agreement between the es- timates and the true dynamics.

Figure 4. Experiment results. (a)-(b) Faraday rotation ob- servations detected by BPD with a random-walk and SE-like magnetic field, respectively. Under such low-SNR conditions, the time-domain magnetic signal is obscured by noise, making commonly-employed time-domain fitting challenging. (c)-(d) Random-walk and SM-like magnetic field generated by the signal generator (gray solid line) respectively, together with the corresponding AEKF estimates (red solid line) obtained without prior knowledge of the field-evolution model.
